text: Diffusion Imaging in Python (Dipy) is a collection of free and open source software projects for analyzing diffusion magnetic resonance imaging (dMRI) data. Diffusion magnetic resonance imaging (dMRI) is an extension of conventional magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) technique and allows probing of certain physical parameters of brain tissue. Many methods have been proposed to infer the architecture of white matter tissue from dMRI data, such as modeling the local properties of white matter fiber bundles and tracking the bundles connecting different regions of the brain. Dipy implements many methods for analyzing dMRI data: diffusion signal processing; reconstruction of diffusion models at the voxel level; fiber tracking, and analysis, and visualization of fiber tracks. Dipy is an attempt to provide clear and uniform interface to provide all methods of diffusion MRI analysis in a uniform programming interface. The code includes classical methods, such as reconstruction of diffusion tensors and deterministic fiber tracking. Novel reconstruction methods, such as constrained spherical deconvolution and diffusion spectrum imaging (DSI) with deconvolution are also implemented, as well as many other novel methods for probabilistic tracking and tractography clustering. Many utility functions for calculating fiber statistics and for visualizing data, as well as file handling are also implemented. While many scientific softwares projects are often the work of a small group of researchers, Dipy is a truly open project. With an interface available on GitHub and an open project mailing list, Dipy is open to anyone to join, whether researcher or developer. The Dipy community has grown to include a large international group of researchers across seven institutions, in five countries, spanning three continents.
explanation: The text is a concise, well-structured technical description with domain-specific terminology typical of documentation. It could be human-written or generated from knowledge about Dipy; there are no obvious personal anecdotes or errors, which keeps ambiguity.
